Hello, hello everybody. Great to see you all. I see some familiar faces. Patrick, good to see you. Built rapport before business. That's great to see. Claudio Paul, Mike here. Good to see you, Scott. Anita. Ian, nice to see you. Hello. Stephen Ketterham, Josep. Fantastic. You're all joining. Great. So we are at the top of the hour. So let me kick off. Welcome everybody to this first session of our mastermind that we launched this month. So welcome everybody. And today we're going to talk about human decision making. And I would like to start with addressing why this is such an important topic. This is the foundation, the fundament of everything we do. Because like I say, if you don't know how people decide to become part of your plan, you probably will be part of their plan. There's a very interesting study done actually in the Netherlands where I'm from. It's about the train. And there are compartments in the train probably like every country where they request you to be silent. So people don't want you to talk there. So what they often do is they put signs like this on the outside of the compartment, or on the inside, you see these icons, these stickers. What the problem was, people kept on talking. So what they did, then they looked, what we need to do, what is my, what were the normal ideas to overcome this problem. Let's put more stickers in the train. Let's do them on every chair. Still, it didn't have the desire effect. And then they asked somebody that had knowledge about behavioral science. How do people really make choices? What is the core of human decision making? And can you address this case from that point of view? And somebody came up with this idea. He designed the compartments where you had to be silent, like a library. And what's the association of being in a library, what people do when they are in a library? Then they are silent. So by having only this association, and as you can see, the icons, the stickers, they were all removed. So the initial prompt to be silent was even removed. Only the fact that the design, the surroundings were different. In this case, they designed the compartment like a library. It was sufficient to get the desired behavior to let people be silent. So therefore, human decision making and understanding it is crucial for your success. So let me first share some foundations. And I see some of our certified coaches as well in this meeting. And perhaps you have already heard these examples. Don't worry. Power of repetition and the reinforcement will be great. Second, by understanding this and bringing this to awareness to your clients, it will drive them as well in your direction. So the first thing I always address is, I want to make three statements about human decision making and why the power of persuasion is so, why to have this skill is so important. And the first one is at Roy, if you see somebody coming into the waiting room, you admit them. Please, thank you. So the first study done was a study done at the University of California. And they asked people, which message do you think would let you start conserving energy? Would that be message one? It helps the environment. Would it be message two? It's good for future generations. So it's good for your children, grandchildren. Would it be message three? It saves money. Everybody loves to save some money, you would assume. Or your neighbors are already conserving energy. So they asked, what do you think would be the most persuasive message for you to start conserving energy? Well, the far majority answered with the message of the environmental protection. And we all encountered this message, where when we go into a hotel, we are asked to reuse our towels to save the environment. You have to be aware that this message is not a guess from these big hotel groups. They have, higher professional agencies to do research and to come up with the most persuasive message. And that message was reuse your towels to save the environment. But in actually, the hotel saved a bit more than only the environment. They saved laundry costs, they saved cleaning costs. So if you imagine a big hotel group with a lot of hotels all over the world, there's millions of saving. So therefore, they hired these professional agencies to come up with the most persuasive message. And probably they spent hundreds of thousands, if not millions of dollars with them. But in reality, what was the finding of this study? When they tested these people, what had the most impact? What message really had the biggest effect was message for. And as you are all studying Dr. Chaldeen's work, you know, the association of message for is social proof. So my claim here is what is crucial to understand if you talk about human decision making, and we will dive deep into this, into this issue, is people typically don't know what influence their behaviors and decisions. Why is this so important? Exactly the reason that I just gave. We often go out there. How do we solve this challenge? How do we solve this influence problem? Let's ask the people that we are talking to what we should do. Science actually tells us you probably don't get the right answers from those people because people typically don't know what influence their behavior. This is a great study that shows this. And of course, this is not the only study. We are all research based, so we never make a claim based on a single study. A multiple study showing that people typically don't know what influence their behaviors. So we need something else. We need an approach, a systematic approach, where we can rely on that we know how people make decisions. Just like the trains in the Netherlands did, what you normally would get as an answer is, well, if you prompt me with those icons that I have to be silent, then probably I will be silent. Well, that didn't do the job. Something else did. The second thing, what is important to understand about human decision making, I go back to the 1930s and I like to introduce you to this man. It's a French sociologist. His name was LaPierre. And LaPierre did a very interesting test. In the 1930s, unfortunately, there was even more racism than nowadays and especially in the United States against Asian people. Asian people were perceived only good enough to work, so they weren't welcome in bars, restaurants, hotels. And LaPierre wanted to visit the United States with two Chinese friends. So he was a smart man. And what he did, he wrote letters. Remember, now we are very used to communicate all over the world like we are doing now. Only in the 1930s, we didn't even have email. So he wrote letters, sent them to 251 hotels, bars and restaurants. And his question was, the hotel, restaurant, bar, I like to visit your establishment with my two Chinese friends. Am I welcome? And out of that 251 establishments, 128 actually wrote a letter back to LaPierre. And the answer was, for him not that surprising, unfortunately, 92% claimed we will reject the Chinese friends. In other words, they said, well, Mr. LaPierre, you're from France, you're welcome, but your Chinese friends are not welcome with us. That was not actually what happened. LaPierre already visited all those 251 establishments with his two Chinese friends. His research was what people actually did. And in reality, he was only rejected once. So 92% said, you are not welcome. But at the moment that people had to make the decision when LaPierre was actually there with his Chinese friends, only one establishment rejected the couple. Or in other words, what is important about human decision making and to a deep level is we don't always act in line with our values. What I want to say here, people can say a lot, I will always do this. Like they say, I would definitely reject the couple. But in reality, they didn't. And recently, I got a great question. They say, but Buzz, if you look at the principle of consistency and commitment, how does that work? Because if people make a commitment to something, they would do it right. That's the whole principle. And that's correct. But how do values, how people often say they have certain values, it's formed in a different way. There's often a lot of peer pressure, social peer pressure in that moment, why people just go with the flow. That's what our principle of social proof says. So they say a lot of things because of that peer pressure. In the moment of decision making, when the peer pressure is taken away, suddenly it aren't their values. Because they didn't make an active or public commitment, what is how we say, you need to activate the principle of commitment. And then the values are gone. Biggest and important lesson for this master's mind is this. You cannot rely if people say, I always do this, I always will do that, that that's the single truth. Research shows a lot of times that people don't always act that way. So that leads us to another thing. The first lesson was, if we want to solve a challenge, we ask those people, we might not get the right answers. Because they don't know what influence their behaviors and decision. The second is, we cannot always rely on if people are standing there and saying, well, I definitely will do that. Again, this leads to, but how do we need to move people in our direction? And I will come there in a second, of course. First, I like to make a third statement. And then I go back to a more recent one at Houston Airport. And what happened there? And this is why I love this case is because this is how it always happens in organizations. Houston Airport had a problem. And that problem was, they got complaints. And no organization likes complaints. The complaint was, it takes way too long before the luggage is at the luggage belt. So they took it very seriously. And I don't know if anybody ever been at Houston Airport. I've been there twice. And it's a huge, huge airport. So compare it with JFK in New York or with, with Heathrow in London. And then what every company normally does, you get a lot of complaints, you dive into the process or something. How can we optimize the process? Because that will solve our complaints. And that's exactly what Houston Airport did. So they hired, again, for a lot of money, experts. And those experts did a fantastic job. They achieved this. Within eight minutes, the luggage was on the luggage belt. So the plane was at the gate. And within eight minutes, the luggage was at the luggage belt. A fantastic achievement because that's much quicker than Heathrow or JFK. So Houston Airport thought, well, we did an amazing job. There won't be any complaints anymore. That was not the case. The complaints kept on coming. So then they looked into another direction to find a solution. They looked into the direction of behavioral science. So again, they hired somebody who had knowledge about human behavior, about human decision making. And that person came with the final solution that really helped. I'll just let you think. Some of you know this answer. But what was the final solution? It was, well, park the plane further away from the gates. Just let people complaining or put some extra entertainment at the luggage belt. Well, if I ask this question to the public, everybody always says answer one or answer three. Luckily, nobody says answer two, because that would be a mistake by definition. You never want to let your clients complain, of course. The right answer here was, let's park the plane further away from the gates. What was the actual case here? These were domestic flights. So no passport checks, no customs. So the domestic flight arrived and the eight minutes were one minute walk, seven minute wait. When they parked the plane further away, it became a seven minute walk and a one minute wait. And there were no more complaints. So the clients were happy and Houston Airport was happy. Now again, what did this cost? Almost nothing. When the plane arrived, they just guided them to another parking spot, so to say. That was the only thing they did. So often, if you understand human decision making at a higher level, you cannot only move more people in your direction, you also will be able to do so with much less investment, lesser time, less money, less energy to reach your goals and to reach your goals even more often. That's why this is such a crucial element of what we do, the science of influence. To do that in the most powerful way, you first have to understand how people really make choices. And my statement here, people don't know what they want. And Henry Ford had a famous saying. He said, if I had to create what people wanted, I had to breed a faster horse instead of inventing the car. Nobody knew at that time they wanted the car. They knew it when they saw it. Same with Instagram. Instagram previously had a slightly different app and nobody wanted it. But suddenly when Instagram was there, everybody wanted it. People also typically don't know what they want. Now, if we notice, we see that as human beings, we are interesting creatures if it comes to decision making. We cannot really predict our own behavior. We cannot give others the information they need to let us move in their direction. Luckily for us, there's almost a hundred years of scientific research telling us how people make decisions and how we can get more yeses out of these people. And to do so, we need to go to the foundations of human decision making. And at this picture, we see two people. And I like to vote are connected actually with Dr. Chaldini. And the first one, one of them would probably all know that's Barack Obama. And the other one, I think some of you might know him by now. Patrick, can you give us the answer? That's Daniel Kahneman, was? Yes, exactly. That's Dr. Daniel Kahneman. And Daniel Kahneman won a Nobel Prize in 2002. And there's the before I dive deeper in Daniel Kahneman, what was the connection of Dr. Chaldini with Barack Obama? And this is not widely known, but it's a very powerful Dr. Chaldini was actually with Daniel Kahneman in the team that drove Barack Obama's election campaigns in 2008 and 2012. And especially in Europe, we see Barack Obama now as this charismatic, great president that the United States had. Of course, in politics, opinions are divided, but that's how he is perceived. But in 2008, he was a pretty unknown senator that suddenly ran for president. And Barack Obama was a smart man. He knew the average playbooks that people have to get into the White House probably wouldn't work for him, because those figures that often run for president are public figures that were in the spotlight for many years at high positions. Of course, Senator is a high position, but he was a quite unknown Senate. And he wasn't the charismatic speaker. We know him nowadays. If you look old interviews from Barack Obama, it's a totally different person almost. So this wasn't the easy job. And Barack Obama realized that. So instead of relying on the standard playbooks that these campaign managers had, he said, I will rely on behavioral science. Well, we all know in the end that was quite the successful choice for Barack Obama. So he won the elections. And Dr. Chaldeany was in the team that left his campaigns. So that's the first connection with Dr. Chaldeany has to this guy's in the picture. The second one is indeed Dr. Daniel Kahneman. Daniel Kahneman, what I'm now going to explain, this is the most fundamental explanation why the principles of persuasion from Dr. Chaldeany are so powerful. Dr. Kahneman won a Nobel Prize in 2002. And he's a behavioral scientist. So you would expect, well, he won the Nobel Prize for psychology. That was not the case because he won the Nobel Prize for psychology doesn't exist. So he couldn't want it. He won the Nobel Prize for economics. And that's an interesting fact by itself, because why does a behavioral scientist win a Nobel Prize for economics? Because of course, the impact that human decision making has on the economy is huge. It's how people spend their money as well. How people make choices has a huge impact on the economy. So what was such an important finding from Daniel Kahneman, which he wrote in his book, Thinking Fast and Slow? He said, in our brain are actually two brains when it comes to human decision making. And luckily for me and all of us, he gave them very easy names. I can remember them. And now I can speak about them. It's system one and system two. We can all remember this. So system one is like a fast unconscious brain. And it works on automatic shortcuts. So it works on shortcuts. They are preprogrammed deeply rooted in a way of decision making. For example, if something is more expensive, it's probably better. If somebody in a hospital wears a white coat, a white coat, he's probably a medical expert. And I can ask him questions about my health and I get a correct answer. These kinds of shortcuts, but it's built for everyday decisions. For example, if somebody approached you in the supermarket, you don't make any difficult calculation. What's the air stream? What's the best angle to pass? You just pass that person. System one takes care of that. So we need system one really hard because research is divided. But they say we make approximately 15,000 to 35,000 decisions per day. And this includes the simple decisions on which chair you set this morning. Where did you put your coat? These are also decisions, simple decisions where you don't really think about when you're doing it. System one is taking care of that for us. So it's an unconscious process. But it's error prone. It's not always the case that these shortcuts are right. It's not always that when something is more expensive, it's better. It's not always the case that when everybody is doing something, it's the best thing to follow. But most of the time it is. So it's error prone, but most of the time these shortcuts guide us into the right direction. Then we have system two. And I compare it with a slow computer. So a slow process is a conscious, rational part of the brain. If you look at the brain, let's say system one is in a core and system two is the outside part of our brain, which makes us humans and makes us the most powerful creature on planet Earth nowadays, because we have the capacity to use system two. There's one problem. System two is effortful. And human beings don't like to put a lot of effort in their decision making. It's also built for complex decisions. And system two can compare all the options that are on the table and then say, well, this is the best option for me. And therefore I select this option and therefore system two is reliable. And now old economics always claims that the human being is a rational creature, right? 2002 Daniel Karaman proved them. So sorry, it's a rational creature. And therefore, when it comes to an important decision, we would use system two. Daniel Karaman proved them wrong. 2002, approximately 90% of all our decisions, including the very important ones, are made with system one. Nowadays, research already shows us that's up to 95%. There's even research that tells that says, but it's not significant enough yet. That is 98%. In a moment, I will explain why this rapid increasing trend is going on. 95% is made based on the shortcuts. Even the most important decisions always give the example of buying a house. Buying a house is one of the biggest decisions a human being makes. What do we do? We start very rational. We go on the internet, make a search. Where do we want to live? How many bedrooms? How many bathrooms? Do I want a garden? Do I want an apartment? And then we filter. Then it comes. We go into view these houses. And then you hear these cliches. Well, it was actually too expensive, but I totally fell in love with the house. I just had to buy it. That's not a rational decision. System one finally made a decision based on totally different shortcuts to buy that house. It were not the facts that drove us into that decision. So a starting point here is, like Daniel Karamal says, system one runs the show. That's the one you want to move. Now I'd like to make some extra deep dive drawings here about system one and two. Because the importance of understanding this is the importance of understanding why these principles are so powerful. So we have system one and system two. Fast shortcut based AeroPro. This is rational brain. It's for complex decisions and it's reliable. Now if we look at it, we already know the far majority of decisions is here. Let's say 95% so far. In a moment I will explain why is there an increasing trend. But there's more to know. Actually system one, here I need to write slow. As it says, system one is fast. The other one is slow. There is research that shows that actually system one makes all the decisions first. It's like 0.3 seconds faster, micro seconds faster than system two. The system one is like making all the decisions because we get all this information overload. So system two, you could also say is more like a handbrake. And I always like to give this example. Let's imagine I walk outside and I see a beautiful little Lamborghini car and then system one thinks, well boss, that's a great car for you. And he wants to buy it. System two is in this metaphor. Literally the handbrake and says, well boss, let's not buy this Lamborghini. That's not the best decision for your bank account. And so far I never bought that Lamborghini. What is the most important lesson here is that system one, let's say never grows up, never becomes smarter. Because what would I've done if I would bought that Lamborghini? I knew it. I know it wasn't the best decision at that moment. But would I tell myself, boss, that was such a dumb decision. You are really stupid that you bought this expensive car. I probably wouldn't do that. What I would do is, well, you work so hard and you like that car so much and you deserve it. You start telling yourself stories like that. So system two, never tell system one that it did something wrong. We call that confirmation bias. So we confirm to ourselves, even if we did something that wasn't the smartest move, that it still was okay to do it. That means that we can rely on that system one will always will be guided by the same shortcuts. That's not that system won't change. So that's the first important lesson here. The second one is we are sitting here in this mastermind together because we want to be more persuasive. And if you want to be more persuasive, you want to use your full brainpower to come up with the best ideas to do so. Now we have a problem because system two, which we will use if we are thinking on campaigns, intent to generate ideas, the ideas that you generate to trigger system two in the other person's mind. And that's a mistake because we all know by now system one runs the show. That's the one you want to move. So based on all these lessons so far about human decision making and knowing system one and system two, we need to be able to use our full brainpower. But to a systematic way of thinking, a systematic process, we need to be able to come up with ideas that will trigger system one in the other person's mind. That's a very powerful thing. You have a process where you can use your full brainpower, but you use it in a way, a systematic way that you can look at a case through the systematic way of thinking, which we teach in our practitioner program. And we also will address here one of the mastermind sessions from a signal. You select the right principle through this principle, you go to a set of ideas based on the activators and amplifiers of those principles. Now you can use your full brainpower, but the set of ideas that you will generate, they do the job because those ideas will trigger the shortcuts and the shortcuts are decision triggers to let people say yes to you. Of course, I will answer questions. So I see people writing great. So if you make notes fantastic, if you have any questions, I will answer them of course as well in this session. So let me get back to here. So the work of Daniel Kahneman is actually a crucial foundation for us to realize, first of all, why are these principles so powerful? Second of all, it brings to awareness that we can rely on science to drive people in our direction because science knows these shortcuts. And third, it tells us we need to have a systematic way to get more yeses, because if I design a campaign without a systematic way, I probably want to persuade people with very rational and factual arguments. That's how the far majority of people tries to persuade another human being. Factual arguments will trigger systems too. And that's not the one that wants to show. This is the core reason why we need to understand the principles of persuasion. And as I promised, why this increasing trend? Why in 2002, 90%, already 95% and already research see we are increasing again. So we rely heavily on system one. Then I introduce you another Nobel Prize winner. Actually, the first behavioral scientist that started to look at behavioral science and connected it with economics, it's Herbert Simon. He won a Nobel Prize in 1978, and they even say he paved the way for Daniel Kahneman to win his Nobel Prize. And Herbert Simon sometimes was even perceived as a bit crazy because he was way ahead of his time. In the 70s, he was already talking about AI, which today for us is true reality. But imagine how people perceived that 55 years ago. What is he talking about? But he was a visionary. In his thesis that won the Nobel Prize, he introduced the term and the term was satisfying. And that's also very important to realize. He said information overload leads to satisfying. Let me ask you a question to think about. In what world do we live in? Do we have more or less information to process than in the 1980s? Well, I can hear you say much more information, more or less than 20 years ago, much more, more or less than 10 years ago, more. We have all these apps on our phones. We have quoted email boxes. We continuous are bombarded with information that we need to process. And that leads to satisfying. What is satisfying? We don't look for the best option anymore. We look for the first sufficiently satisfactory solution. If you now compare this to System 1 and System 2, what's the lesson here? System 2 is designed in our brain to choose the best option. If we have satisfying, we stop doing that. So that's one of the reasons why I say we lean more and more on System 1 because of all the information overload. Our slow computer just cannot process all that information good enough. So to pick the best option every time, we go into a mode of satisfying and we ask ourselves a question, not anymore. Is this the best option for me? Is this sufficiently good enough? And again, what are the decision triggers? To answer that question, not the facts. The facts will trigger System 1. These shortcuts that are pre-programmed in every human being, in every culture, in every language, they will answer that question for us. If it's sufficiently good enough, yes, because the expert says so. Yes, because it scares inevitability. Yes, because everybody is doing so. That's how we answer the question. Not yes, because this new car has a motor engine of 3.6 liters and 22-inch wheels, which I really love. That are not the final decision triggers. The other things will do the job. So that's one reason why we rely more heavily on System 1, the information overload. The funny thing, we always think nowadays, oh, we are much smarter than people 100 or 150 years ago. Yes, we are able to do great things with technology that they weren't able to do. But if it comes to decision making, those people had more time to make the best decision than we nowadays have. They use System 2 to make the best option more often than we do. Why information overload leads to satisfying? And a funny example of satisfying is this research done in the US. And people after a hard day of work, you realize, phew, I need some groceries for evening dinner. How do you walk into the supermarket with all your brain power on and let's look for the best special offer today? Probably not. You just go in, you see some things, take it, and you want to go. And a lot of people go in that mode because of a lot of information overload, their mind is completely somewhere else with that state of mind in the supermarket. Therefore, they did this experiment. They had these small stickers, about this size, with everyday low price. And in the morning, the managers of the supermarket just randomly put those stickers to certain products. The next day, in the evening, they take them away and the next day, they assign those stickers. Totally random to different products. Again, they did that for three weeks, and then they looked if a product had such a sticker. What is the increase in the amount of sales? It was astonishing 100%. The sales doubled if the product had a sticker everyday low price. Even the sticker itself doesn't say anything. It doesn't say special offer, it's just confirming, well, everyday this product has a low price. A 100% increase. Why? We are guided by System 1 who is perceiving these things. Okay, apparently, is this sufficiently good enough? Yes, because this product everyday has a low price. If you racially analyze this, it's almost insane that we do these things. But this is the core of our human decision making. We perceive ourselves, and nobody likes to look in the mirror or self-reflect and think, well, am I really doing that? We like to look at ourselves as super rational. Maybe the other people on planet Earth, well, they do this behavior, boss. I know me, I always make the best decisions. And that also makes it very hard for us to accept that we all are this. We've unconsciously pushed against this that we are this. But we are this, and again, this is a hardcore reason, trust on the science because there's so much of scientific research on this. Then the point is, last one, why is System 1 increasing? I go back to Canadian research done in 2005 with brain scanning devices. And you see here in an iconized form two creatures that live on planet Earth. One is the human being and one is a goldfish. And if I tell you, a goldfish has an attention span of nine seconds. And a human being has had an attention span of about 20 minutes in the 1980s. How many minutes do you think the attention span of a human being was in 2005? I see some people moving that way. Yes, definitely low. Sometimes I hear five minutes, two minutes, sometimes two minutes. It's eight seconds. We were beaten by the goldfish. This became actually a bit controversial research because the thesis was written in a way that human beings cannot have the attention to something longer than eight seconds. That's of course not the case. We can watch a movie for one and a half hour or a Netflix series. But luckily, Microsoft repeated this research in 2010. From 2005 to 2010, Microsoft found attention span dropped from eight to seven seconds. And what it is, is how quickly we are distracted. And I always like to give this example is you take out your phone because you want to call somebody. You open your screen and you see you have text messages, Facebook messages, Instagram notifications. Bang! Within one second we are distracted because what we do, we are not going to call the person. We open those messages. In one second we are gone. Then after we answered all those messages, then sometimes people are with a phone in their hand and thinking, what was I going to do? Why is this so important relating to our increasing reliance on system one? System two, as I said, it costs us effort. To do something that costs effort, you need to keep your attention in that area. We are so quickly distracted that it becomes more and more difficult to use system two. And therefore we rely as well on system one. So this is also to do with that satisfying information overload. That's why our attention span is also dropping, but it comes from another angle and therefore we rely more heavily. Then system one is always often compared to our croc brain, reptile brain, croc brain. Especially in online communication, I like to compare, I think it's a wrong comparison. I like to compare system one to a child of seven instead of to a crocodile for two reasons. One system one is definitely smarter than the average crocodile. System one knows two plus two is four. System one can drive a car at an empty road. Did you never had that you drove a car, you come at your destination, you think, how did I get here? Well, probably system one was driving. So system one is smarter, more important, and especially in an online world, is if you think how to communicate to somebody, it's easier to think how to communicate to a child of seven than how to communicate to a crocodile. So to do a few simple tests, these are real cases where I was involved. And please write your answers in the chat, open it here, which email do you think was opened more knowing we need to trigger system one? Is that answer A or B, $100 straight into your bank account? Or I see already an A in the chat, some A's, the best students ever here. I see all A's. Thank you for your participation. And that's the right answer because it's simpler language, so easier to process. I never heard a parent say to that child, well, let's go to the shop because we can have this cashback promotion. No child would know what that is. Then this one is also interesting, which Ad got more clicks. This is a campaign, as you can see, which you did in China. And the difference is the picture of the woman. Do you think Ad A or B? I see B's A, the majority is B and B is indeed the correct answer. Why emotions? System one is also drawn to strong emotions. If you have an imagine on a birthday and the adults are laughing real loud and there's a little child, probably it starts laughing as well. It has no clue why it's laughing, but it's attracted to that emotion. So a first thing, some tips that I want to share. If you want to talk to System one, use simple language. Use as little words as possible. Again, definitely in the online world. Make it visual because we can process visuals much quicker than words and make it emotional because we are drawn to emotions. Then the last promise I made to all of you. I told both people had a connection with Dr. Chaldini at this picture. And what is the connection that Daniel Kaneman had with Dr. Chaldini? Of course, Daniel Kaneman had a lot of questions. Well, respect here is your Nobel Prize, great job, fantastic work. But tell us, now we know we need to move System one. How on earth do we do that? And there's a story which literally happened. Dr. Chaldini and Dr. Kaneman were in the same room with a lot of grad students. And Dr. Chaldini came to the students where Daniel Kaneman was talking. And Dr. Chaldini made a light joke. And he says, Oh, there is the man. And then Daniel Kaneman stood up and looked at the students. He says, No, no, Dr. Chaldini is the man. So Daniel Kaneman always said, if you want to move System one, you need to see the principles that Dr. Chaldini defined, the universal principles of influence as deep triggers that will trigger System one, so more people will move in your direction. And just the principles are for some of you who first joined this mastermind, reciprocity, liking, unity, social proof, authority, consistency and scarcity. Yes, I see familiar faces that also are in a practitioner or certified coach program. You will be trained in that program for that systematic approach. What we are going to do now, everybody has the foundation and the right way of thinking about human decision making. In this mastermind, we will dive deep into every principle in all little nuances with examples that are not in the practitioner program. You will work on your cases, your challenges for you, for your clients, applying these principles. And therefore, in my mind, this was a crucial not to be missed session to understand why it's so powerful, why we need a systematic approach to use our full brainpower to come through these principles to a set of ideas that will move System one, and therefore we will hear more yeses. In other words, from now on, if we want to hear yes, we start to see these principles as the decision triggers in the people we want to move in our direction. One short chapter. It's connected to ethics. Everything we do is connected to ethics. Then I will take some questions as well. I want to make an important claim because this affects human decision making. Persuasion is not misleading. You can mislead people once, but there's also something to do with human decision making. If you mislead one person once, they will make totally different decisions to you or in association with you if you mislead them. So also in our training, we give you three things to think about. We always say, and I will add in this session, three more things in the online environment. First one, are you truthful? Do you tell the truth? Would I tell this to my father, mother, brother or sister? This information. These are the minimum criteria, by the way. Am I presenting my request sincerely? Or in other words, there's a story about a supermarket who says, we have a special deal with our waters. It's normally 69 cents. They had a for sale sign and the new price was 69 cents. More people bought the water because like the six stickers, every day low price. But at one moment, people perceive that message and understand they are being fooled. So it will backfire enormously. That's what we mean. Do I present my requests sincerely? My data sincerely? And maybe the most important question of all is, is it wise to make this request? If a person would say yes to me today, would they say yes to my next request? And if one of these three questions is answered with no, I and everybody in the Chaldeany Institute would advise you don't make that request. You may get your yes today, but it will probably backfire and you won't get any more yeses. And that person will probably tell a lot of people don't do business with that individual. But in the online world, we have other ways of unethical designs, how I call them. So one question you also should ask, am I not using a shape or pattern in a different way than in Ten? For example, this you see a lot on websites. They design like an email box or a message icon that appears you have a message. Yes, more people click on it because this batch app icon, it's a very powerful prompt. But it's unethical. It's clickbait. Don't do that. Two, and I'm not deliberately creating confusion. For example, check this box if you don't want to receive a newsletter. You check a box if you want something, not if you don't want something. Still, I see a lot also big companies that use these techniques. There's more and more legislation now coming up to avoid this kind of behavior. So you maybe win once, but you not win on the long run if you use these kinds of designs. And my third one is, am I not hiding something important? For example, people do it very small forms. If you click continue, suddenly this in this example, this will become your starting page. People don't want that. So don't hide information on purpose. So ethical persuasion and human decision making. So they make decisions in the long run. And in the short, though you have success in the short term and long term on human decision make, tell the truth, be sincere, offering real value and present your design without creating confusions and hiding important aspects. I want to thank you for your attention in this mastermind session about human decision making. Does anybody has a question? How that works in the menu? There's a button reactions and there you can raise your hand and I will take your question. You can unmute yourself if there are any questions or comments. Yes, Flavio. Good to see you. Ciao, Bas. So when people are quickly distracted, it became a more complicated area of course than 20-30 years ago. What can I best advise in this session Flavio is can you form what is the behavior that you want to achieve? What do you call engagement? Because I worked in a lot of consulting areas where a lot of companies ask me the same question. I want to increase my engagement and I say that's something as you say, I want more sales. But sales is not a behavior that I can influence. Engagement is not the behavior that I can influence. So what I like to do and I'm not a guy as you know, I want to answer questions with questions. But what I would recommend is if you can get a measurable behavior. Engagement is opt-in for a newsletter. Engagement is the open rate of the email. Engagement is the click-through rate after an email. Engagement is the reply rate on my LinkedIn messages. If we can start defining that, that's the first step. Because engagement contains a lot of different areas, I just now named four. And then per type of engagement, we need to have an influence case. And per influence case, we can see which kind of principles would suit that case the best. So that would be my approach. And I'm happy that during these mastermind sessions, and as you are being a Chaldeany certified coach to continue this conversation. Would that make sense, Flavio? Yeah, I think you gave a good hint. Remember, reading your book and getting this approach of breaking down into the engagement into something more, let's say, solid or an action, a real action to follow. Define the behavior. That's, and that's, that's the thing I always advise. We cannot influence the outcome, we can influence the behavior that leads to the outcome. And therefore, the more specific we can be about what we want to achieve. And then we achieve it. Like I said, I did this multiple times within small and large organizations. And everybody nowadays wants the attention from their clients. And often it's called engagement. Only you can define multiple ways of engagement. And that is difference per company, per person, what is really your goal. So once we define that, we can move forward and have this journey here together. And start with one. That's also my advice. I like this example. Thanks for sharing, your honor. Great job. Thanks a lot. Shrikant. Yeah, I have a typical example, right? I work in a technology setup when we make software, right? And then, so there are a lot of managers, you know, those are very timid and would not actually do the thing in the right time or the right way and would not agree to certain suggestions, which are the right suggestions. And they get stuck and the whole thing gets delayed because of that. How do you influence something like that? Fantastic question. And again, these principles work, work fantastic for it. So a thing, a quick one, what I, what is an experience with managers, I always reflect to the principle of consistency, because managers and people high up in organizations, normally intend to believe themselves. They build their career by making certain decisions and they came higher up. And the more higher up they came, the more they start thinking, I make the best decision. So one tip I can get is look where they made a certain decision, connecting with the decision they want, you want them to make today. Of course, all the principles work, but that's the first advice that comes to mind, which often people, let's call it higher up in the organizational hierarchy, are quickly affected by. So I hope that helps. Thank you. Yes, it does. Fantastic.
